symbols: raised arms — desperate prayer and supplication to God from the depths
tattered green robe — the roughness of the prophet’s garments and his ordeal
carved reliefs in the background — the civilization of Nineveh to which Jonah was sent
upward gaze — the prophet’s turning back to God after fleeing
description: A full-length figure of a muscular man in a tattered dark green robe, standing with both arms raised high above his head and his face turned upward in a cry of intense emotion—anguish, supplication, or praise. His body is taut with physical effort. Behind him, a wall displays carved stone reliefs depicting various scenes. The palette is muted golds and greens. The figure dominates the composition with raw physical and emotional energy.
theological_description: The prophet Jonah, depicted here in a moment of impassioned prayer, is one of the most dramatically human figures in the Old Testament. Called by God to preach against Nineveh, Jonah fled in the opposite direction (Jonah 1:3), was swallowed by a great fish, and from its belly cried out: ‘Out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice’ (Jonah 2:2). His raised arms may represent either this prayer from the depths or his subsequent reluctant preaching to Nineveh, which resulted in the greatest mass repentance in Scripture (Jonah 3:5-10). Jesus identified Jonah’s three days in the belly of the fish as the definitive sign pointing to his own death and resurrection: ‘For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth’ (Matthew 12:40). The reliefs in the background evoke the Assyrian civilization of Nineveh, the great enemy city to which God’s mercy extended—scandalizing Jonah but revealing that God’s compassion knows no national boundaries.

scripture_refs.reason: Jonah’s prayer from the belly of the fish, the cry of deliverance depicted in his raised arms
As Jonah was three days in the fish, so the Son of Man will be three days in the earth
Nineveh’s repentance, the Assyrian civilization evoked by the background reliefs
God’s call to Jonah and the prophet’s initial flight from his mission
Jonah’s anger that God is gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in love
